one_line_takeaway: A survey arguing that LLMs and formal verification fail on opposite kinds of code, so hybrid 'LLM-proposes, verifier-disposes' pipelines are the promising path for vulnerability detection—illustrated by a 0.73s ESBMC proof versus a ~12.7h blow-up on two toy C programs.
key_finding: The two paradigms fail on complementary inputs, motivating hybrids. On a nested-loop integer-summation program, all three LLMs incorrectly flagged CWE-190 (integer overflow), while ESBMC proved no overflow (VERIFICATION SUCCESSFUL) in 0.73 seconds. On a program summing 0..10^9, an LLM trivially recognizes no vulnerability, but ESBMC must unroll the loop 10^9 times: unwinding the first 1,000,000 iterations took 46 seconds, extrapolating to ~12.7 hours (with very high memory) to fully verify. The survey concludes hybrid pipelines (LLM proposes, formal tool disposes) combine FV's soundness/low false positives with LLMs' broad, cross-language pattern coverage.
core_contribution: A comprehensive survey/overview (arXiv:2503.10784v1, cs.SE, Mar 2025) of software vulnerability detection organized around three paradigms: (1) classical formal verification (FV), especially bounded model checking (BMC); (2) LLM-based analysis (fine-tuned encoders and prompt-based decoder-only models); and (3) emerging hybrid pipelines that couple LLMs with FV/static tools. The authors' three self-stated contributions are all survey-level: an analysis of FV-based methods and their scalability/language-coverage limits, a critical assessment of LLM-based approaches, and an exploration of hybrid strategies. The only ORIGINAL empirical artifact the paper itself produces is a small motivating experiment on two hand-written C programs (Figure 2) showing complementary failure modes of LLMs vs FV tools. NOTE: the headline systems discussed (PropertyGPT, ESBMC-AI, LEMUR, SecureFalcon, CryptoFormalEval, FVEL) are CITED prior work reviewed in the survey, NOT this paper's own contributions.
threat_model: Not an adversarial threat-model paper; it is a methodological survey of defensive software-security tooling. The security context is detecting and repairing software vulnerabilities (memory-safety bugs, integer/arithmetic errors, injection/XSS, smart-contract flaws such as reentrancy) and reporting them via CWE identifiers, spanning SAST/DAST/IAST/fuzzing/pentesting/FV. The paper does not define an attacker capability model, adversarial-robustness setup, or its own attack; it does note (from cited work) that small behavior-preserving code transformations can mislead LLM detectors.
limitations: As a v1 survey it is qualitative and non-systematic: no stated search protocol, inclusion criteria, or PRISMA-style methodology, and the first-party experiment is anecdotal (2 programs, 3 models, no statistics). Coverage skews toward the authors' own ecosystem (ESBMC, ESBMC-AI, FormAI/FormAIv2, SecureFalcon). Reported numbers for hybrid systems are taken from the cited papers rather than independently reproduced, so cross-system claims are not head-to-head. FV limitations noted by the authors themselves: BMC misses classes like SQL injection/XSS, suffers state explosion, and most FV tools cover only C/C++/Java (Python recently added).

datasets_used: First-party experiment: 2 hand-written C programs (Figure 2) — one nested-loop integer sum, one 0..10^9 sum

Catalogued in Table 1 (surveyed, not used by this paper): Draper (1274k, C/C++, static-analyzer-labelled/less reliable), SARD (450k, C/C++/Java/PHP), SeVc/SySeVR (420k, C/C++), DiverseVul (349k, C/C++, real-world), FormAIv2 (331k, C, AI-generated), BigVul (264k, C/C++), PrimeVul (235k, C/C++), VulDeeLocator (198k, LLVM IR), Juliet C/C++ (64k), Code Gadgets (61k), Juliet Java (28k), FELLMVP (15k, Solidity), CVEfixes (12k), ChatPHP (2.5k, AI-generated), VCMatch (1.6k), SVEN (1.6k), Ponta2019 (1.2k, Java), VulBench (455), Ullah2023 (228), Smartbugs (143, Solidity), MAGMA (138), Vulcorpus (100, Java)
quantitative_results: ESBMC proved no integer overflow on the summation program in 0.73 s (VERIFICATION SUCCESSFUL); all of ChatGPT-4o, o1, and DeepSeek R1 wrongly reported CWE-190 [first-party]
ESBMC unrolling 0..10^9 loop: first 1,000,000 iterations = 46 s; full 10^9 unroll extrapolated to ~12.7 hours with very high memory [first-party]
Prompt-based direct querying of advanced code models: ~54.5% balanced accuracy; naive scaling/prompting did not help much [cited]
Fine-tuning is the dominant adaptation approach, ~73% of recent studies [cited]
SecureFalcon: 121M parameters (derived from Falcon-40B), outperforms BERT/RoBERTa/CodeBERT and traditional ML [cited]
PropertyGPT (GPT-4, smart contracts): 80% recall of ground-truth properties and 12 bug-bounty-earning zero-day flaws [cited, NDSS 2024]
ESBMC-AI self-healing pipeline: evaluated on 50,000 vulnerable C programs from the FormAI dataset [cited]
baselines_compared: First-party motivating study: ChatGPT-4o vs OpenAI o1 vs DeepSeek R1 (all incorrect on program 1) vs ESBMC formal model checker (correct)
Surveyed architecture families: encoder-only (CodeBERT, GraphCodeBERT), encoder-decoder (CodeT5), decoder-only (GPT series, CodeLlama)
Surveyed models/tools discussed as cited baselines: ReGVD, VulBERTa, SecureFalcon, PropertyGPT, ESBMC-AI, LEMUR, CryptoFormalEval, FVEL
